topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The air in the ballroom was thick with expensive perfume and forced merriment. I kept my gaze fixed on the intricate floral arrangements, a deliberate shield against the inevitable. Then, across the room, a familiar silhouette caught my eye. Liam. He was laughing, his arm draped casually around a woman whose smile seemed effortlessly radiant. My stomach tightened, a cold knot forming just beneath my ribs. I watched him, noting the easy confidence he exuded, and a sharp, unpleasant clench seized my jaw. I forced a slow sip of champagne, the bubbles doing little to soothe the sudden prickle of inadequacy that washed over me. I felt clumsy, an intruder in this scene, acutely aware of my own ill-fitting dress and the way my hands kept fidgeting with my clutch. When our eyes briefly met, there was a flicker—a recognition quickly shuttered, replaced by a desperate urge to melt into the wallpaper.

[story 2]

I’d rehearsed my entrance into the wedding reception in my head a hundred times, a mental script designed to keep me small and invisible. But when I spotted him near the buffet table, the rehearsed lines dissolved into a sudden, panicked silence. He looked up, and for a split second, the practiced composure I’d built around my face crumbled. I could feel my cheeks heating up, a flush spreading from my neck all the way to my hairline. Every movement felt exaggerated—too quick, too jerky. I managed a strained smile, a brittle thing that didn't reach my eyes. When he approached, his tone was warm, and the ease with which he spoke about his life felt like a spotlight shining directly onto my perceived shortcomings. I mumbled a greeting, my voice sounding unnaturally high, and immediately looked down at my shoes, wishing the polished floor would swallow me whole.

[story 3]

The music swelled, a vibrant, overwhelming sound that seemed to amplify the distance between me and everyone else. I was trying to appear engaged, nodding at conversations I didn't quite grasp, but my focus kept drifting back to the corner where he stood. Seeing him there, looking so settled and happy, brought a sharp, internal wave of deflation. My shoulders involuntarily hunched up toward my ears. I noticed the way I was holding my glass—too tightly, the condensation slick against my fingers. When he finally turned his attention toward me, there was a moment of suspended reality. I felt a distinct urge to retreat, to simply vanish into the periphery. I managed a nod, a gesture that felt hollow, and then I turned abruptly toward the exit, needing the stark, empty space of the hallway to escape the uncomfortable pressure of being observed.
